title: When k, p and t go soft
rule: Consonant gradation: kauppa → kaupassa, katu → kadulla, Helsinki → Helsingissä
summary: Finnish words change their own consonants when you add an ending. kauppa becomes kaupassa, katu becomes kadulla, Helsinki becomes Helsingissä - the k, p or t in the middle weakens. This is why a word you know perfectly well can look unrecognisable with a case on it, and why learners often think they've met a new word when they haven't. For the big everyday group drilled here - words like kauppa, katu, tyttö, ranta, whose plain form is already the strong grade - the weak grade turns up with -n, -ssa, -lla and -lle, while the partitive and the 'going to' form keep the strong one. Watch out for the smaller group that runs the other way: words ending in -e, -as or -in (osoite, opas, rakas) are WEAK in their plain form and strengthen instead - osoite becomes osoitteen, opas becomes oppaan. So 'plain form = strong' is a rule about this set, not about Finnish.

items.prompt: Say you're AT the shop (pp → p)
items.note: kauppa → kaupassa: the double pp thins to a single p. Spoken Finnish then clips -ssa to -s, so you hear 'kaupas'.
nk → ng. It's a spelling change AND a sound change - 'Helsingissä' has the ng of 'singer', not a hard k. This is the single most common place learners freeze mid-sentence.

items.prompt: Say you're AT the bank (kk → k)
items.note: kk → k. Same shape as kauppa → kaupassa: a doubled consonant loses one half.

items.prompt: Say you're ON the street (t → d)
items.note: A single t weakens all the way to d: katu → kadulla. Finnish d only really exists as a weak-grade t, which is why it's rare at the start of words.

items.prompt: Say it's ON the table (t → d again)
items.note: pöytä → pöydällä. Front vowels take -llä rather than -lla - vowel harmony and gradation apply at the same time, independently.
p → v: leipä → leivän. Not an obvious one to guess, and it shows up constantly in everyday words - leipä, halpa → halvan, apu → avun.

items.prompt: Say it happens IN the evening (lt → ll)
items.note: lt → ll: ilta → illalla. The t assimilates completely into the l before it.

items.prompt: Say you're AT the beach (nt → nn)
items.note: nt → nn, the same assimilation as lt → ll. Compare also rt → rr: kerta → kerran.
tt → t. The -n ending here is the genitive - 'the girl's' - and genitive always pulls the weak grade.

items.prompt: Say it's on the rug (tt → t)
items.to_en: It's on top of the rug.
items.note: matto → maton. Worth saying aloud next to 'mato' (a worm), which has one t all the way through - the length is the whole difference between the words.

items.prompt: Now go the OTHER way: partitive keeps it strong
items.note: Going TO somewhere restores the strong grade: kaupas → kauppaan. Weak and strong aren't 'before and after' - each ending picks its own, and you learn which by meeting them.

items.prompt: Strong grade again - the partitive never weakens
items.note: This is where gradation and the partitive meet: kaupassa is weak, but kauppaa is strong. Same word, two endings, two grades - which is exactly why the partitive drills and this one belong next to each other.
